text: It may be said that natural selection is daily and hourly scrutinising, throughout the world, every variation, even the slightest; rejecting that which is bad, preserving and adding up all that is good; silently and insensibly working, whenever and wherever opportunity offers, at the improvement of each organic being in relation to its organic and inorganic conditions of life.
author: Charles Darwin
source: Project Gutenberg plain text, eBook #1228 (1859 First Edition)
out_pulls_note: Out-pulls the gloss: 'silently and insensibly working... daily and hourly scrutinising' dramatises the blind, designer-less mechanism far more vividly than the abstract 'design without a designer' one-liner.
search_notes: Gutenberg #1228 is explicitly the 1859 FIRST edition (the ebook header lists #1228=1859 1st, #22764=1860 2nd, #2009=1872 6th). Confirmed it is the 1st ed. by the absence of 'survival of the fittest' (introduced 5th ed.). Passage is in ch. IV 'Natural Selection'. Byte-verified verbatim. Canonical Darwin; no misattribution risk.
